title: Command arguments

title: Command arguments > Overview
summary: This section explains the concept of command arguments in the context of executing commands. Arguments are positional and must be provided in a specific order, unlike flags which can be in any order. They are used for required inputs with a natural order.
facts: Arguments are positional values provided after the command name.
Arguments must be provided in the exact order defined in the command class.
Flags can be specified in any order, unlike arguments.
Arguments are suitable for required inputs with a natural order.
chunk_text:
Title: Command arguments > Overview
Summary: This section explains the concept of command arguments in the context of executing commands. Arguments are positional and must be provided in a specific order, unlike flags which can be in any order. They are used for required inputs with a natural order.
Facts:
- Arguments are positional values provided after the command name.
- Arguments must be provided in the exact order defined in the command class.
- Flags can be specified in any order, unlike arguments.
- Arguments are suitable for required inputs with a natural order.
Entities: arguments (concept), flags (concept)
Relationships: arguments -contrast-> flags
Actions:

section_id: content/guides/ace/args.md#Command arguments > Defining your first argument
title: Command arguments > Defining your first argument
summary: This section explains how to define command arguments in Ace using class properties with the `@args` decorator. It provides an example of creating a command that greets a user by name.
facts: Command arguments are defined as class properties with the `@args` decorator.
The order of properties in the class determines the order of arguments.
String arguments are defined using the `@args.string` decorator.

examples.explanation: Defines a command that greets a user by name using a required string argument.

chunk_text:
Title: Command arguments > Defining your first argument
Summary: This section explains how to define command arguments in Ace using class properties with the `@args` decorator. It provides an example of creating a command that greets a user by name.
Facts:
- Command arguments are defined as class properties with the `@args` decorator.
- The order of properties in the class determines the order of arguments.
- String arguments are defined using the `@args.string` decorator.
Entities: @args (function), @args.string (function), GreetCommand (class), BaseCommand (class), name (other)
Relationships: GreetCommand -inherits-> BaseCommand
Actions: define command arguments, run command with name argument
Examples:
- import { BaseCommand, args } from '@adonisjs/core/ace'

export default class GreetCommand extends BaseCommand {
  static commandName = 'greet'
  static description = 'Greet a user by name'
  
  /**
   * Define a required string argument
   */
  @args.string()
  declare name: string

  async run() {
    this.logger.info(`Hello, ${this.name}!`)
  }
}
Defines a command that greets a user by name using a required string argument.
Warnings: 
Prerequisites:

title: Output: Hello, John!
summary: This section explains the behavior of Ace when a required argument is not provided by the user.
facts: Ace displays an error message if a required argument is missing.

section_id: content/guides/ace/args.md#Output: Hello, John! > Accepting multiple values
title: Output: Hello, John! > Accepting multiple values
summary: This section explains how to accept multiple values for a single argument in a command using the `@args.spread` decorator in AdonisJS. The spread argument must be the last argument to capture all remaining values.
facts: The `@args.spread` decorator allows a command to accept multiple values for a single argument.
The spread argument must be the last argument in the command.
The example command 'greet' can accept multiple names and greet each one.

examples.code: node ace greet John Jane Bob
examples.explanation: Runs the 'greet' command to greet multiple users by name.

chunk_text:

Summary: This section explains how to accept multiple values for a single argument in a command using the `@args.spread` decorator in AdonisJS. The spread argument must be the last argument to capture all remaining values.

- The spread argument must be the last argument in the command.
- The example command 'greet' can accept multiple names and greet each one.
Entities: @args.spread (function), GreetCommand (class), BaseCommand (class), args (module)
Relationships: GreetCommand -inherits-> BaseCommand, GreetCommand -uses-> @args.spread
Actions: import BaseCommand, import args, declare names, run GreetCommand
Examples:
- node ace greet John Jane Bob
Runs the 'greet' command to greet multiple users by name.

section_id: content/guides/ace/args.md#Hello, Bob! > Customizing argument name and description
title: Hello, Bob! > Customizing argument name and description
summary: This section explains how to customize the argument name and description in Ace commands. By default, Ace converts property names to dashed-case for argument names. You can override this with the `argumentName` option and add a description for clarity.
facts: Default argument names are in dashed-case format.
The `argumentName` option customizes the argument name.
Descriptions help users understand the required input.

examples.explanation: Customizes the argument name and adds a description.

chunk_text:
Title: Hello, Bob! > Customizing argument name and description
Summary: This section explains how to customize the argument name and description in Ace commands. By default, Ace converts property names to dashed-case for argument names. You can override this with the `argumentName` option and add a description for clarity.
Facts:
- Default argument names are in dashed-case format.
- The `argumentName` option customizes the argument name.
- Descriptions help users understand the required input.
Entities: argumentName (config), description (config)
Relationships: argumentName -used with-> description
Actions: customize argument name, add description
Examples:
- @args.string({
  argumentName: 'user-name'
})
declare name: string
Customizes the argument name to 'user-name'.
- @args.string({
  argumentName: 'user-name',
  description: 'Name of the user to greet'
})
declare name: string
Customizes the argument name and adds a description.
Warnings: 
Prerequisites:

title: Hello, Bob! > Making arguments optional
summary: This section explains how to make command-line arguments optional in AdonisJS by setting the `required` option to `false`. It also highlights the importance of ordering optional arguments after required ones to avoid parsing issues.
facts: All arguments are required by default in AdonisJS commands.
Optional arguments must be placed after required arguments.
The `required` option can be set to `false` to make an argument optional.

actions.object: required option to false to make an argument optional

examples.explanation: Runs the greet command with only the required argument.

chunk_text:
Title: Hello, Bob! > Making arguments optional
Summary: This section explains how to make command-line arguments optional in AdonisJS by setting the `required` option to `false`. It also highlights the importance of ordering optional arguments after required ones to avoid parsing issues.
Facts:
- All arguments are required by default in AdonisJS commands.
- Optional arguments must be placed after required arguments.
- The `required` option can be set to `false` to make an argument optional.
Entities: GreetCommand (class), BaseCommand (class), args (module)
Relationships: GreetCommand -inherits-> BaseCommand, GreetCommand -uses-> args
Actions: set required option to false to make an argument optional
Examples:
- node ace greet John
Runs the greet command with only the required argument.
Warnings: 
Prerequisites:

section_id: content/guides/ace/args.md#Output: Good morning, John! > Providing default values
title: Output: Good morning, John! > Providing default values
summary: This section explains how to provide default values for optional arguments in Ace commands, ensuring that a default value is used when no input is provided by the user.
facts: Default values can be specified for optional arguments using the `default` property.
If no value is provided by the user, the default value is used.
The argument becomes optional but always has a string value.

examples.explanation: This code declares an optional argument with a default value of 'guest'.

chunk_text:
Title: Output: Good morning, John! > Providing default values
Summary: This section explains how to provide default values for optional arguments in Ace commands, ensuring that a default value is used when no input is provided by the user.
Facts:
- Default values can be specified for optional arguments using the `default` property.
- If no value is provided by the user, the default value is used.
- The argument becomes optional but always has a string value.
Entities: default (config), args.string (method)
Relationships: args.string -uses-> default
Actions: specify default value for optional arguments
Examples:
- @args.string({
  description: 'Name of the user to greet',
  required: false,
  default: 'guest'
})
declare name: string
This code declares an optional argument with a default value of 'guest'.
Warnings:

section_id: content/guides/ace/args.md#Output: Hello, guest! > Transforming argument values
title: Output: Hello, guest! > Transforming argument values
summary: The `parse` method in the `@args.string` decorator is used to transform or validate command-line argument values before they are assigned to class properties. This is useful for normalizing input, converting types, or performing validation.
facts: The `parse` method is used to transform or validate argument values.
It receives a raw string value from the command line.
The method must return the transformed value.
In the example, the `parse` method converts the input name to uppercase.

examples.explanation: This code snippet demonstrates how to use the `parse` method to convert a user's name to uppercase before it is used in the command.

chunk_text:
Title: Output: Hello, guest! > Transforming argument values
Summary: The `parse` method in the `@args.string` decorator is used to transform or validate command-line argument values before they are assigned to class properties. This is useful for normalizing input, converting types, or performing validation.
Facts:
- The `parse` method is used to transform or validate argument values.
- It receives a raw string value from the command line.
- The method must return the transformed value.
- In the example, the `parse` method converts the input name to uppercase.
Entities: parse (method), @args.string (function)
Relationships: parse -used in-> @args.string
Actions: transform argument value, validate argument value, convert name to uppercase
Examples:
- @args.string({
  argumentName: 'user-name',
  description: 'Name of the user to greet',
  parse(value) {
    return value ? value.toUpperCase() : value
  }
})
declare name: string
This code snippet demonstrates how to use the `parse` method to convert a user's name to uppercase before it is used in the command.
Warnings: 
Prerequisites:

title: Output: Hello, JOHN!
summary: The section explains how to use the parse method for validating input values in a TypeScript command file. It provides an example of validating an email address by checking for the presence of an '@' symbol and converting the email to lowercase.
facts: The parse method can be used for input validation.
An error is thrown if the input value is invalid.
The example checks for an '@' symbol in an email address.
The email address is converted to lowercase if valid.

examples.explanation: Validates an email address by checking for '@' and converts it to lowercase.

chunk_text:
Title: Output: Hello, JOHN!
Summary: The section explains how to use the parse method for validating input values in a TypeScript command file. It provides an example of validating an email address by checking for the presence of an '@' symbol and converting the email to lowercase.
Facts:
- The parse method can be used for input validation.
- An error is thrown if the input value is invalid.
- The example checks for an '@' symbol in an email address.
- The email address is converted to lowercase if valid.
Entities: parse method (method), commands/create_user.ts (file)
Relationships: parse method -used in-> commands/create_user.ts
Actions: use parse method, throw error, convert email to lowercase
Examples:
- @args.string({
  description: 'Email address of the user',
  parse(value) {
    if (!value.includes('@')) {
      throw new Error('Please provide a valid email address')
    }
    return value.toLowerCase()
  }
})
declare email: string
Validates an email address by checking for '@' and converts it to lowercase.
Warnings: 
Prerequisites:

section_id: content/guides/ace/args.md#Output: Hello, JOHN! > Accessing all arguments
title: Output: Hello, JOHN! > Accessing all arguments
summary: This section explains how to access all command-line arguments in an AdonisJS command using the `this.parsed.args` property. This feature is useful for debugging and inspecting arguments.
facts: The `this.parsed.args` property allows access to all command-line arguments as a key-value object.
The `GreetCommand` class is used to greet a user by name.
The command outputs a greeting message using the provided name.

examples.explanation: Logs all arguments as a key-value object.
Outputs a greeting message using the provided name.

chunk_text:
Title: Output: Hello, JOHN! > Accessing all arguments
Summary: This section explains how to access all command-line arguments in an AdonisJS command using the `this.parsed.args` property. This feature is useful for debugging and inspecting arguments.
Facts:
- The `this.parsed.args` property allows access to all command-line arguments as a key-value object.
- The `GreetCommand` class is used to greet a user by name.
- The command outputs a greeting message using the provided name.
Entities: GreetCommand (class), this.parsed.args (concept), BaseCommand (class), args (module)
Relationships: GreetCommand -inherits-> BaseCommand, GreetCommand -uses-> this.parsed.args
Actions: access all command-line arguments, output greeting message
Examples:
- console.log(this.parsed.args)
Logs all arguments as a key-value object.
- this.logger.info(`Hello, ${this.name}!`)
Outputs a greeting message using the provided name.
